items.q: What three notes form a major triad in root position?
items.why: Students think 'three notes = a triad'. Triads are specific: stacked thirds from the root. C major = C, E, G (skipping D and F). Consecutive scale notes are clusters, not triads.

items.q: Which chords form a plagal cadence?
items.why: Students confuse plagal (IV-I) with perfect (V-I) because both end on I. The 'amen' at the end of hymns is the classic plagal cadence - subdominant to tonic, with a softer ecclesiastical feel.

items.correct: A cadence ending on chord V, creating a sense of incompleteness.
items.misconception: A cadence that sounds wrong or is performed badly.
items.random_wrong: A cadence using only major chords.
items.why: Students think 'imperfect' means flawed. It's a technical term for an unfinished-sounding cadence - any chord moving to V leaves the music suspended, awaiting resolution.

items.q: Which cadence moves V to vi?
items.why: It's Interrupted. “Perfect” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In a first inversion chord, the bass note is:
items.why: Correct answer: The 3rd. “The root” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many cadence types are commonly taught at GCSE (perfect, plagal, imperfect, interrupted)?
items.why: Exact answer: 4. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
